text:
Ms. Adair, a venerable Scotsman by descent and a learner all her life, ran a vocational program that taught young people to chisel stone. Her own maternal grandmother had been an elite craftswoman, and Ms. Adair was loth to let that backbone of skill fade. "Compare any chemical shortcut to honest labor," she would say, "and you will return to the chisel." Her exterior was stern, yet her cognitive frankness made students less envious than grateful.

The systematic curriculum had a predictable, seasonal pattern. In the warm months students worked outdoors until sunburn forced them under the bungalow roof beside the plantation that bordered the school. Each pupil learned to subtract waste from a block, to pave a path, and to react calmly when a piece cracked. The endurance this demanded was no small extent of their training; an unfortunate slip could ruin a week's effort, and the disillusion that followed tested even the most prepared.

One immediate problem that protracted into the eighth and seventh week was a dearth of good mineral stone. The consultant they hired, a frank elderly builder, plugged a lamp into a wall socket near the temple-shaped studio, unrolled a sketch, and spoke with exalted certainty. "This region was once a hinterland of quarries," he said. "The industry here could pave the way for a true revival. Let imagination, not mere want, guide you."

He was a namesake of a long-dead sculptor, a coincidence that delighted the dormitory at night. The students would compare their work, mourn a failed bud of an idea, and feel a strange sensation of awe at how a plump lump of clay could, once shaped, redound to their pride. To avail themselves of every chance, they categorized each tool and stowed it with care, lest a careless mishap miscarry the whole project.

Winter brought ultraviolet warnings and thinning ozone in the news, plus a tax debate that reached even this quiet northern town. Ms. Adair remained obedient to her own rule: beauty first, profit later. She would not let the dead weight of difficult bookkeeping crush the feeling of joy. When a wealthy patron offered to reimburse the cost of marble shipped from Oceania, she accepted, then used part of the cannon-shaped fountain commission to buy cosmetics and small wares as prizes.

The final show was magnificent. An orchestra played while a balloon drifted over the courtyard and a single liquid line of light traced each finished statue. Critics from as far as the upper coast came; some, plainly envious, called the work a prophecy of a new school, comparing it to the calcium-white classical busts of old. Ms. Adair only smiled. Her plasma of ambition had never been about fame. The fascination she felt, watching a once-obedient student grow bold, was reward enough. She picked up a fresh ware, examined a sneeze-inducing cloud of dust in the ambient light, and, ineffective tools set aside, named her favorite piece after the nostril-flaring lad who had carved it, sealing his adoption into the long, proud line of her craft. History, she liked to say, would vindicate the slow trades: while empires plunder and armies jam the roads in panic, while waves of emigration empty whole villages, a center of patient skill endures. She paid the marble bill in a single instalment, refused to categorize art by price, and taught each pupil to stow a tool and to name a stone with equal respect, knowing how far one lesson could ramify through a life.
translation:
阿黛尔女士，祖籍是位令人尊敬的苏格兰人，一生都是个学习者，她开办了一个职业培训项目，教年轻人雕凿石头。她自己的外祖母曾是一位精英级的女工匠，而阿黛尔女士不愿让那一脊梁般的技艺消逝。「把任何化学捷径同诚实的劳作相比，」她常说，「你最终都会回到凿子这边来。」她外表严厉，但她在认知上的坦率，让学生们与其说嫉妒，不如说感激。

这套系统化的课程有着可预测的、随季节而变的规律。温暖的月份里，学生们在户外劳作，直到晒伤迫使他们躲进与学校相邻的种植园旁那栋平房的屋顶下。每个学生都要学会从石块上削去多余的部分，铺一条路，并在作品开裂时冷静应对。这所需要的耐力，是他们训练中不小的一部分；一次不幸的失手可能毁掉一周的努力，而随之而来的幻灭，连最有准备的人也会受到考验。

一个拖延到第八周和第七周的紧迫问题，是优质矿石的匮乏。他们聘请的顾问是位坦率的年长建筑工人，他把一盏灯插进神殿状工作室旁墙上的插座，摊开一张草图，带着得意的笃定说道。「这一带从前是采石场的腹地，」他说。「这里的产业能为一场真正的复兴铺平道路。让想象力——而非单纯的匮乏——来引导你们。」

他与一位早已去世的雕刻家同名，这个巧合在夜里的宿舍里令大家欣喜。学生们会比较彼此的作品，为一个夭折的念头之芽哀叹，并对一团丰满的黏土一经塑形竟能增添他们的自豪而感到一种奇异的敬畏。为了不放过任何机会，他们给每件工具分类，小心收存，以免一次粗心的意外把整个项目搞砸、运不到目的地。

冬天带来了新闻里的紫外线警告和臭氧层变薄的消息，还有一场连这个安静北方小镇都波及到的税收辩论。阿黛尔女士仍然服从她自己的规则：美在先，利在后。她不会让繁难记账的沉重负担压垮那份喜悦的感觉。当一位富有的赞助人提出报销从大洋洲运来的大理石费用时，她接受了，随后用那笔大炮形喷泉委托项目的一部分款项买了化妆品和小物件作为奖品。

最后的展览十分壮观。一支管弦乐队演奏着，一只气球飘过庭院，一道液体般的光线勾勒出每一尊完成的雕像。远至上游海岸的评论家都来了；有些人显然心怀嫉妒，称这些作品是一个新流派的预言，把它们比作旧时钙白色的古典半身像。阿黛尔女士只是微笑。她那如血浆般的雄心从来都不是为了名声。看着一个曾经顺从的学生变得大胆，她所感到的入迷，已是足够的回报。她拿起一件新出炉的作品，在环境光中端详那团令人打喷嚏的尘埃，把低效的工具搁在一旁，用那个鼻翼张大的小伙子——正是他雕出了这件作品——的名字为自己最喜爱的作品命名，将他的「收养」封进她那条悠长而骄傲的技艺传承之中。她爱说，历史终将为这些缓慢的行当正名：当帝国掠夺、军队惊慌中堵塞道路，当一波波向外移民掏空整座村庄时，一个耐心技艺的中心却能长存。她一次付清了大理石的账单，拒绝按价格给艺术分类，并教每个学生以同样的敬意去收存一件工具、去为一块石头命名，因为她深知一堂课能在一生中分叉延展到多远。
